name: Dharma-Sankata Frame — The Crisis at Kurukshetra
proposition: The battlefield of dharma is the site where the entire epic's moral tension crystallizes; Arjuna's refusal names the crisis
repetition_rationale: Frame verse 1.1 — the single opening that names the entire epic's tension; BG returns to dharma-crisis at 2.7 and 18.72
echo_hypothesis: Search for passages where a warrior or king names a moral impasse using dharma-sankata language before a sage intervenes with teaching

name: Moral Paralysis — Visada as Entry Condition
proposition: Arjuna's grief and bodily collapse at the sight of kin is the necessary prior condition for all teaching that follows
repetition_rationale: First and only occurrence of the collapse itself; echoed structurally whenever a hero in MBH weeps before receiving wisdom
echo_hypothesis: Scenes where a warrior prostrates in grief and a teacher responds with instruction — the viSAda-upadesha pattern

name: Kulakshaya Cascade — Destruction of Family Destroys Dharma
proposition: When lineages are destroyed, eternal family-dharmas perish, women are corrupted, varna-samkara arises, and ancestors fall
repetition_rationale: Arjuna's own argument, used ironically — BG does not endorse it but the MBH narrative takes it seriously as a social concern
echo_hypothesis: Passages in AdiP and StrIparva where lineage-destruction and its dharmic consequences are mourned

name: Armies and Conch-Blowing — The Epic Stage
proposition: The cataloguing of warriors and the cosmic sound of conch-shells establishes war as a cosmic event, not a personal quarrel
repetition_rationale: The first half of ch1 — narrative setup that makes Arjuna's collapse more striking
echo_hypothesis: Battle catalogues and conch-blowing formulae across MBH war narratives

name: The Wise Grieve Not — Rebuke of Conventional Grief
proposition: Krishna rebukes Arjuna's grief as unworthy of a wise person; the learned grieve neither for the living nor the dead
repetition_rationale: Stated at 2.11 (opening rebuke) and restated at 2.30 after the full atman argument; grief-rebuke is the BG's rhetorical opening
echo_hypothesis: MBH scenes where a sage explicitly tells a grieving hero that grief is unbecoming the wise; contrast with legitimate grief in StrIparva

name: Indestructibility of the Self — Atman is Unborn and Undying
proposition: The atman is unborn, eternal, undying, ancient — it cannot be slain when the body is slain
repetition_rationale: BG's foundational metaphysical argument — stated, expanded, and restated across 2.12-2.30; the most elaborated node in chapter 2
echo_hypothesis: Any MBH passage using aja/nitya/avyaya to describe the self or Brahman; especially philosophical discourses in Shantiparva

name: Body-Self Distinction — Dehin and Deha
proposition: The embodied self (dehin) passes through childhood, youth, and old age in the body (deha); death is merely change of garment
repetition_rationale: Repeated to anchor each phase of the atman argument: dehin-deha at 2.13, antavanta-dehe at 2.18, garment metaphor at 2.22
echo_hypothesis: The garment/body metaphor for death in MBH narrative; kings and sages using zarIra-dehin language in philosophical asides

name: Tolerance of Opposites — Titiksha
proposition: Sense-contacts bring pleasure and pain as cold and heat — they are impermanent; the wise one must endure them
repetition_rationale: First statement; elaborated later as sama/equanimity in 2.38, 2.48, 6.7; titiksha is a distinct virtue — endurance before equanimity
echo_hypothesis: MBH descriptions of kshatriya or muni enduring extremes — tapas in heat and cold, svarga descriptions using zItoSNa language

name: Sat and Asat — Being and Non-Being
proposition: Non-being has no existence; being has no non-existence — the boundary of both is seen by knowers of truth
repetition_rationale: Single verse but philosophically the densest — the ontological foundation; echoed in 13.12, 17.26 (sat/asat usage in different registers)
echo_hypothesis: Philosophical asides in MBH where sat/asat are used as technical ontological terms, particularly in Shantiparvan moksha-dharma

name: Ascharya — The Marvellous Nature of the Self
proposition: Some see the self as a marvel, some speak of it as a marvel, some hear of it as a marvel — yet having heard, none truly know it
repetition_rationale: Single aphoristic verse; its rhetorical form (fourfold parallel) suggests it is a pre-existing wisdom saying being cited
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using Azcaryavat about the self or the divine — this may be a floating formula across parvas

name: Svadharma and the Warrior's Duty
proposition: For a kshatriya, no greater good exists than righteous battle; such a war comes of itself as an open gate to heaven
repetition_rationale: Stated at 2.31 as duty-argument and reinforced at 2.33 as consequence-argument; structurally a two-step persuasion
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where sages or elders invoke svadharma and kshatra-dharma to persuade hesitant warriors before battle

name: Equanimity in Success and Failure — Samatvam
proposition: Yoga is equanimity; perform actions established in yoga, having abandoned attachment, equal in success and failure
repetition_rationale: 2.38 pairs victory/defeat; 2.48 defines yoga itself as samatva — the definition is given after its application
echo_hypothesis: MBH sages and kings described as sama in victory and defeat; especially Yudhishthira's post-war equanimity discourse

name: Buddhi Yoga — Single-Pointed Resolute Intelligence
proposition: The resolute intelligence is one-pointed; the irresolute has infinite branching thoughts; buddhi yoga alone frees from karma-bondage
repetition_rationale: 2.41 defines the single vs. many-branched intelligence; 2.44 shows distracted intelligence cannot settle in samadhi; 2.50 identifies buddhi yoga as skill in action
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages contrasting the resolute and irresolute mind; discourses on buddhi as distinct from manas in Shantiparva

name: Transcendence of Vedic Ritual — Beyond the Three Gunas
proposition: The Vedas deal with the three-guna domain; be beyond the three gunas, free from pairs, ever in sattva, not seeking acquisition
repetition_rationale: BG critiques the vedavadins — those who think Vedic ritual exhausts the spiritual; the well-simile at 2.46 is the summary
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where karma-kanda ritual is contrasted with jnana or moksha paths; Shantiparva debates on ritual vs. knowledge

name: Sthitaprajna — Portrait of the Steady-Minded Sage
proposition: The sthitaprajna has abandoned all desires from the mind, is content in the self alone, unmoved in sorrow, not exulting in pleasure, free of passion, fear, and anger
repetition_rationale: The entire 2.54-72 section is a sustained portrait; four distinct criteria repeated: desire, emotions, senses, and ocean-simile; brAhmIsthiti at 2.72 is the culminating term
echo_hypothesis: MBH descriptions of rishis or kings using these exact qualities — vItarAga, vItabhaya, vItakrodha as a formulaic triad; especially Bhishma's descriptions of sages in Shantiparva

name: Desire-Chain — From Thought to Ruin
proposition: Contemplating sense-objects generates attachment; from attachment arises desire; from desire anger; from anger delusion; from delusion memory-loss; from that destruction of intelligence; from that the person perishes
repetition_rationale: Single two-verse sequence but one of the most quoted in the entire BG; a causal chain — re-echoed at 3.37-41 in expanded form
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages (especially Yaksha-prashna, Shantiparva) where the causal sequence of kama-krodha-moha is explicitly stated

name: Nishkama Karma — Act Without Attachment to Fruit
proposition: You have a right only to action, never to its fruits; let not fruit be your motive; let not inaction be your refuge
repetition_rationale: 2.47 is the core verse; 3.19 restates for the karma-yoga argument; 4.20 gives the liberated-while-acting form; this is the BG's most repeated practical teaching
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages — particularly Janaka's teaching, Vyasa's discourses — on acting without desire for fruit

name: Compulsory Action — No One Can Be Without Action
proposition: Not even for an instant can anyone exist without performing action; all are compelled to act by the gunas of prakriti
repetition_rationale: 3.5 states the compulsion; 3.8 restates as prescription — do the necessary action; the argument counters the sannyasa-as-inaction misunderstanding
echo_hypothesis: MBH philosophical passages arguing against pure inaction; debates in Shantiparva about whether the sannyasin truly ceases all action

name: Yajna as Cosmic Reciprocity — The Wheel of Sacrifice
proposition: Creation was joined with sacrifice by Prajapati; by this shall you prosper; this is the wish-granting cow; the wheel thus set in motion must be kept turning
repetition_rationale: 3.10 introduces cosmic yajna; 3.14-15 gives the anna-parjanya-yajna-karma-brahman chain; 3.16 names non-participation as sin
echo_hypothesis: MBH cosmological passages linking yajna, rains, food, and cosmic order; creation myths in Adiparva where Prajapati establishes sacrifice

name: Lokasangraha — Acting for the Welfare of the World
proposition: The wise person should act for the welfare of the world without attachment, having regard for the cohesion of peoples
repetition_rationale: 3.20 gives the Janaka example; 3.21 states the general principle; 3.25 contrasts attached and unattached action for the same social goal
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a king or sage cites responsibility to the world as motive for action despite personal exhaustion

name: Gunas Act — Not the Self — False Doership
proposition: In all cases it is the gunas of prakriti that act; one deluded by ego thinks 'I am the doer'
repetition_rationale: 3.27 diagnoses the delusion; 3.28 gives the corrective view of the tattvavit; elaborated again at 5.8-9 and 13.29
echo_hypothesis: Shantiparva moksha-dharma passages where the sage explicitly denies doership

name: Desire as the Enemy — Kama the Great Devourer
proposition: It is desire, it is anger, born of rajas, all-devouring, greatly sinful — know this as the enemy here
repetition_rationale: 3.37 names desire-anger; 3.38 gives three metaphors (smoke/fire, dust/mirror, membrane/embryo); 3.43 concludes with the hierarchy
echo_hypothesis: MBH moral discourses explicitly naming kama as enemy using mahAzana or duSpUra imagery

name: Sense-Desire Hierarchy — Intellect Above Senses
proposition: Above the senses is mind; above mind is intellect (buddhi); above intellect is the Self — knowing the Self above intellect, subdue the self by the Self
repetition_rationale: Culminating verse of ch. 3; the hierarchy recurs in Sankhya framework across BG; echoed at Katha Upanishad 3.10-11
echo_hypothesis: Sankhya-style hierarchies in MBH philosophical discourses listing sense-mind-buddhi-atman

name: Guru-Parampara — The Eternal Teaching Passed Down
proposition: This imperishable yoga was proclaimed to Vivasvat, by Vivasvat to Manu, by Manu to Ikshvaku — the royal seers thus knew it, but it was lost by time
repetition_rationale: Sets the BG within cosmic time and lineage — this teaching is not new; positions it as recovery of lost wisdom
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages citing the Manu-Ikshvaku lineage; passages where an ancient teaching is said to have been lost and recovered

name: Avatara — Divine Incarnation to Restore Dharma
proposition: Whenever dharma declines and adharma rises, the divine manifests age after age for protection of the good and destruction of the wicked
repetition_rationale: The divine descent as response to dharmic crisis — which is precisely the situation of the MBH itself
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where Krishna's incarnation purpose is stated or acknowledged by other characters

name: Knowing Divine Birth Liberates
proposition: One who truly knows the divine birth and action, having shed the body does not return to rebirth
repetition_rationale: The soteriological payoff of the avatara doctrine — knowledge of divine birth itself liberates
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where knowing Krishna's true nature is described as liberating

name: All Paths Lead to Me — Reciprocal Divine Response
proposition: In whatever way people approach the divine, the divine rewards them in that way — all paths are valid
repetition_rationale: One of the most universalist declarations in BG — all paths are valid; distinct from bhakti-exclusivism
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages affirming the validity of multiple paths to the same goal

name: Varna is by Guna-Karma — Not Birth
proposition: The fourfold varna-system was created according to guna and karma — not by birth
repetition_rationale: Varna defined by guna/karma not birth — the BG's implicit critique of birth-based caste
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where varna is attributed to guna/karma rather than jati; Vanaparva debates and Shanti-Anushasana on Brahmin qualities

name: Action in Inaction — The Paradox of the Free Actor
proposition: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is wise among humans — a yogi who has accomplished all action
repetition_rationale: 4.18 states the paradox; 4.19-22 give three modes of the free actor
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a sage acts in the world but is explicitly said not to be bound — Janaka narratives

name: Jnana-Yajna — Knowledge as the Highest Sacrifice
proposition: As fire reduces fuel to ash, the fire of knowledge reduces all actions to ash; no purifier equal to knowledge exists
repetition_rationale: 4.24 (brahmarpanam), 4.33 (jnana-yajna supremacy), and 4.37 (fire metaphor) form a triptych
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using jnana-agni or jnana-yajna as a metaphor; fire of knowledge burning karma in sage biographies

name: Seek the Teacher — Guru-Seva for Knowledge
proposition: Know that by prostration, question, and service; the knowers of truth will teach you knowledge
repetition_rationale: The guru-shishya protocol stated explicitly; echoed throughout MBH as the standard opening of philosophical dialogues
echo_hypothesis: MBH philosophical discourse scenes using the praNipAta-pariprazna-sevA triad

name: Doubt as the Root of Ruin
proposition: The doubt-natured one perishes; for the doubting self there is neither this world nor the next nor happiness
repetition_rationale: Both the diagnosis (doubt destroys) and the cure (cut doubt with sword of knowledge) in a single passage
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a hero's doubt is identified as the root problem — notably Yudhishthira's recurring doubts

name: Sannyasa and Yoga Lead to the Same End
proposition: Both renunciation and yoga lead to the highest good; of the two, karma yoga is superior to renunciation without yoga
repetition_rationale: 5.2 asserts both paths lead to highest good; 5.4-5 states Sankhya and yoga see the same
echo_hypothesis: MBH debates between sannyasins and active warriors about which path is superior; Suka-Janaka dialogue

name: The Non-Doer in Action — Senses Move Among Senses
proposition: The yogi who knows truth thinks 'I do nothing at all'; the senses move among sense-objects like a lotus leaf untouched by water
repetition_rationale: 5.8-9 give the exhaustive list of actions performed without doership; 5.10 gives the lotus-leaf metaphor
echo_hypothesis: MBH descriptions of sages active in the world but untouched — Janaka, Shuka — using lotus and water metaphors

name: Brahma-Nirvana — Extinction in Brahman
proposition: The sage with inner joy, inner delight, inner light, being Brahman, attains brahma-nirvana
repetition_rationale: brahmanirvANa appears at 5.24, 5.25, 5.26 — three consecutive verses; the term bridges Vedantic and Buddhist vocabulary
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using brahmanirvana or brahmabhuta as the goal of the sage

name: Samadarshin — Equal Vision Across All Beings
proposition: The pandits see with equal eye the Brahmin, the cow, the elephant, the dog, and the dog-eater
repetition_rationale: The most radical social statement in ch. 5 — equal vision includes the outcaste; directly enacts ekam-sat
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a sage is praised for treating all beings equally; Yaksha-prashna; Chandala episodes

name: Krishna as Enjoyer of All Sacrifice — Sarvalokamaheshvara
proposition: Having known Krishna as the enjoyer of all sacrifices, the Lord of all worlds, the friend of all beings, one attains peace
repetition_rationale: Ch. 5 culminating verse — synthesizes the three roles: receiver of yajna, ruler of all worlds, friend of all
echo_hypothesis: MBH hymns addressing Krishna or Vishnu using these three epithets

name: Sannyasa Equals Yoga — No Yoga Without Renouncing Sankalpa
proposition: What they call sannyasa, know that as yoga; no one becomes a yogi who has not abandoned sankalpa
repetition_rationale: Ch. 6 opens by collapsing the sannyasa/yoga distinction — the real renunciation is of intention
echo_hypothesis: MBH debates on true vs. false sannyasa

name: Self as Both Friend and Enemy
proposition: Let the self elevate the self; let not the self depress the self; the self alone is the self's friend, the self alone the self's enemy
repetition_rationale: The reflexive self-relationship — not an external enemy but the untamed mind itself is the obstacle
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages on jita-atman and the inner enemy; descriptions of ideal kings and sages

name: The Yogi's Portrait — Sama in All Conditions
proposition: The yogi who sees clod, stone, and gold as equal, is steady, sense-controlled — said to be truly integrated
repetition_rationale: The kuta-stha portrait — unmoved as a peak — alongside the extreme equality (clod = stone = gold)
echo_hypothesis: MBH character descriptions using samaloSTa-ashma-kanchana or kuta-stha as praise of sages

name: Dhyana Method — The Actual Practice of Meditation
proposition: The yogi should practice seated, in a clean place, holding body neck and head erect, gazing at the nose-tip, mind controlled, established in brahmacharya
repetition_rationale: The only fully systematic description of meditation posture and technique in the BG; prescriptive not descriptive
echo_hypothesis: MBH ascetic descriptions using the same technical posture vocabulary; Vanaparva tapas descriptions

name: Mind as the Restless Enemy — Abhyasa and Vairagya
proposition: The mind is restless, turbulent, powerful — yet by practice and dispassion it is restrained
repetition_rationale: Arjuna's doubt articulates the universal problem; Krishna's answer (abhyasa + vairagya) is the complete practical prescription
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages using the formula abhyasa-vairagya as the two-limbed path

name: The Fallen Yogi — Continuity Across Births
proposition: The yogi who falls from yoga is not lost — reborn in a pure or wise family, the previous practice carries forward
repetition_rationale: The reassurance to the incomplete yogi — effort is never wasted across births
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages on rebirth carrying forward spiritual merit; stories of sages reborn with memory of previous attainment

name: The Yogi is Supreme — Above Ascetics, Scholars, and Ritualists
proposition: The yogi is superior to ascetics, scholars, and ritualists — therefore be a yogi
repetition_rationale: A ranking verse — BG places yoga above tapas, jnana, and karma separately
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages ranking paths — tapas vs. jnana vs. yajna vs. yoga; Shantiparva comparative debates

name: Bhakta-Yogi as the Highest Yogi
proposition: Among all yogis, the one devoted with inner self gone to Me, worshipping with faith — that one I consider the most integrated
repetition_rationale: Ch. 6 culminating verse — the bhakta-yogi tops the hierarchy; bridges dhyana to devotion
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a devotee-sage is ranked above mere ascetic or scholar

name: Vision of Self in All Beings — Sarvabhutastham Atmanam
proposition: The yogi established in oneness worships the divine abiding in all beings — sees the self in all and all in the self
repetition_rationale: 6.29-6.32 — four consecutive verses consolidating the ekam-sat vision; the clearest statement of the project's central thesis
echo_hypothesis: MBH passages where a sage sees the one in all — Shantiparva descriptions of brahmajna sages and Narayaniya section
